abstract:
Throughout the 20th century, numerous scholars cautioned against the use of static, ontological, and metaphysical interpretive frameworks rooted in Hellenistic philosophy, noting their restrictive impact on Christian conceptions of the divine. In light of contemporary spiritual and philosophical shifts—specifically the rise of apatheism and anatheism—the relevance of traditional theological categories must be re-examined within the field of pastoral theology. This paper proposes a fundamental paradigm shift, moving away from classical "omni-categories" toward "pathos-categories" to more effectively address the complexities of human suffering, dignity, and justice. I argue that theopaschitic theology provides a vital mechanism for reframing rigid God-images inherited from the Greco-Roman tradition. By introducing the concept of a "sustainable God," this study reconfigures God’s compassionate presence (*ta splanchna*) as a continuum of infinite interventions, characterized by "infiniscience" and a pneumatology of "being-with."

**Keywords:** Pastoral theology, theopaschitism, pathos-categories, suffering, divine compassion, anatheism.
